title: Foundations & I/O
description: Build an unshakeable C++ foundation: variables, data types, cin/cout, operators, and the mental model for thinking in C++. These concepts are the scaffold upon which every competitive programming solution is built — master them until they are completely automatic.

sections.content: Every competitive programmer starts from a template. This is not laziness — it is efficiency. Your goal in a contest is to **think about algorithms, not boilerplate**. Memorize this template until you can type it from muscle memory in under 10 seconds.

sections.title: The Standard Competitive Programming Template

rows: Includes the ENTIRE C++ standard library in one line
No need to remember individual headers like `<vector>`, `<algorithm>`, etc.
Saves keystrokes in every line of output/input
Unsyncs C and C++ I/O streams
Makes `cin`/`cout` dramatically faster — essential for large input
Prevents flushing `cout` before every `cin` — another speed boost

sections.label: Critical Warning
sections.content: After adding `ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false)`, **do NOT mix `scanf`/`printf` with `cin`/`cout`** in the same program. The desync will cause undefined output order. Pick one and stick with it.

Choosing the **right data type** is a competitive programming skill. Using `int` when you need `long long` causes silent overflow — one of the most common bug sources in contests. Know your type sizes.

rows: When you need positive-only huge numbers

sections.label: Golden Rule for Overflow
sections.content: If a problem says n ≤ 10⁵ and you're computing n² somewhere, that's up to 10¹⁰ — which overflows `int` (max ~2×10⁹). **Always use `long long`** when intermediate computations can exceed 10⁸. The cost is negligible; the bug-savings are enormous.

sections.title: Overflow Demonstration

sections.content: C++ is **fast but unforgiving** — uninitialized local variables contain **garbage values** (random memory content). This causes mysterious bugs that are very hard to track down. Always initialize.

sections.title: Declaration Best Practices

sections.content: Efficient I/O is **not optional** in competitive programming. A slow I/O routine with the correct algorithm can still TLE (Time Limit Exceeded). Master `cin`, `cout`, and their formatting.

sections.title: I/O Patterns You Must Know

sections.label: \n vs endl Performance
sections.content:
`endl` flushes the output buffer every time it's called — this is **significantly slower** than `'
'`. In a loop printing 10⁵ lines, `endl` can cause TLE. Always use `'
'` or `"
"` in competitive programming.
Operator mistakes cause silent wrong answers. The most dangerous ones are **integer division**, **operator precedence surprises**, and **bitwise vs logical operators**.

sections.title: Critical Operator Behaviors

sections.content: Codeforces problems almost always involve **T test cases**. Know this pattern by heart — it appears in virtually every contest problem.

sections.title: Standard Multi-Test Case Template

sections.label: The solve() Pattern
sections.content: Always put your logic in a separate `solve()` function. This ensures clean variable scope — variables from one test case cannot accidentally interfere with the next. It's the standard professional CP pattern.

sections.title: Exercise 1: Integer Overflow & Long Long
sections.explanation: Line 1: `int * int` = 10,000,000,000 which overflows `int` (max ~2.1 billion), producing garbage value `1410065408`. Line 2: casting `a` to `long long` forces long long multiplication — correct result. Line 3: using `100000LL` suffix forces long long arithmetic — also correct. Always use `LL` suffix or cast when multiplying large numbers.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Operator Precedence Trap
sections.explanation: `x++` prints 5 (post-increment: use then increment, x=6). `++x` increments first to 7, then prints 7. `10/3=3` (integer division). `10%3=1`. `-10%3=-1` (C++ modulo: sign follows dividend, NOT divisor).

sections.title: Exercise 3: Read N Numbers and Print Their Sum
sections.approach: Use the standard template. Read `n`, then loop `n` times reading each value with `cin >> val`. Accumulate into a `long long sum`. Print sum. Use `\n` not `endl`.
sections.explanation: Note the use of `long long sum` — even if each value fits in `int`, their sum might not. This is a common competitive programming pattern. The `ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false)` + `cin.tie(NULL)` is crucial when N is large (e.g., 10⁵ values).

sections.title: Exercise 4: T Test Cases — Min and Max
sections.approach: Use the multi-test-case `solve()` pattern. Read N, then the N values. Track min/max using `INT_MAX`/`INT_MIN` sentinels, or use `min()`/`max()` from `<algorithm>` (included via `bits/stdc++.h`).
sections.explanation: `INT_MAX` is ~2.1 billion — guaranteed larger than any value, so the first comparison always updates `mn`. Similarly `INT_MIN` is ~-2.1 billion. Using the `solve()` function guarantees clean variable scope per test case. `min()` and `max()` are from `<algorithm>`, automatically included via `bits/stdc++.h`.

items: **Types**: Use `long long` whenever values can exceed ~2×10⁸ in intermediate computation.
**Overflow**: `int * int` can overflow silently. Cast to `(long long)` or use `LL` suffix.
**Division**: `-7 / 2 = -3` in C++ (truncates toward zero, NOT floor). `-7 % 2 = -1`.
**I/O Speed**: Always use `'\n'` instead of `endl`. `endl` flushes and is 10–100x slower.
**Uninitialized**: Always initialize local variables. Uninitialized C++ variables contain garbage.
**Multi-test**: Use `void solve()` pattern with `while(t--)` loop for T test cases.
**Bitwise**: `n & 1` checks odd/even. `n << 1` doubles. `n >> 1` halves. `(l+r)>>1` is a safe midpoint.
